Purpose: Genotype QC, format conversion, filtering, basic relatedness and PCA
Specifics: The Swiss-army knife of human-style popgen. PLINK2 handles biobank-scale data and has improved relatedness routines. Read your VCF in here first. Pretty much every other tool in this list assumes data that has been through PLINK at some point.
Issues and warnings: Default filters are tuned to humans - check MAF and HWE thresholds carefully for non-model species. PLINK2 file formats are not fully back-compatible with PLINK 1.9.

Purpose: VCF filtering, summary statistics, and conversion
Specifics: Use bcftools for anything you'd type at the shell - filtering, normalising, splitting multi-allelics, computing per-site statistics. VCFtools is older but still widely used for site-frequency spectrum and Tajima's D outputs. Combine with PLINK for the QC step before any popgen analysis.
Issues and warnings: Multi-allelic and complex variants need careful normalisation (`bcftools norm`); otherwise downstream popgen statistics can silently break.

Purpose: Popgen statistics, PCA, admixture, and selection from genotype likelihoods
Specifics: If your data is below ~10x coverage, work in genotype-likelihood space rather than calling genotypes. ANGSD computes the SFS, FST, theta, and Tajima's D directly from BAM files. PCAngsd does PCA and admixture from ANGSD output and is the standard for low-coverage datasets.
Issues and warnings: Tutorials are not always current - check GitHub issues for the latest practice. Reference allele bias matters for very low coverage and for ancient DNA.

Purpose: First-pass description of population structure
Specifics: Always start here: a PCA plot (smartpca, SNPRelate, or PCAngsd) plus ADMIXTURE at a range of K values, evaluated with EvalAdmix to flag mis-fit. This is the minimal-effort, maximal-information first look at any new dataset. fineSTRUCTURE and conStruct are the next steps if structure is complex.
Issues and warnings: Do not interpret a single best K from ADMIXTURE - inspect a range and use cross-validation plus EvalAdmix residuals. PCA is sensitive to relatedness and missing data; LD-prune first.

Purpose: Standard tools for phasing and IBD detection
Specifics: Beagle for phasing (or SHAPEIT5 at biobank scale), then hap-ibd to detect long IBD segments. The output of hap-ibd feeds directly into IBDNe / HapNe-IBD for recent demography. For low coverage or aDNA, replace this stage with GLIMPSE2.
Issues and warnings: Phasing accuracy depends strongly on sample size and reference panels. Switch errors fragment IBD segments and bias downstream Ne estimates - validate on simulations matched to your sample size.

Purpose: Phase and impute low-coverage and ancient DNA
Specifics: If your data is low-coverage WGS or aDNA, this is the entry point. GLIMPSE2 if you have a relevant haplotype reference panel (humans, domesticated species, anything in 1000G-style resources). STITCH if you don't - it imputes from the sample itself by inferring K founder haplotypes jointly across all samples, which is the default choice for non-model organisms, conservation genomics, and quantitative-genetics designs. Output then feeds into the standard PCA / ADMIXTURE / IBD workflow.
Issues and warnings: GLIMPSE2: reference-panel ancestry match dominates accuracy. STITCH: tune K and sample size; accuracy depends on within-sample haplotype diversity. Both: filter on INFO score and genotype probability before downstream analyses.

Purpose: Quick non-parametric Ne(t) estimate from the SFS
Specifics: Cheap, fast, no model specification needed - run this first to see the rough shape of demographic history before committing to PSMC / SMC++ / msprime-based inference. Works on the folded SFS so no outgroup is needed.
Issues and warnings: Cannot model migration or splits. Recent-time resolution is poor (use HapNe / GONE2 / IBDNe). Sensitive to genotype-calling biases - use after careful QC.

Purpose: Reconstruct Ne(t) from sequence data
Specifics: PSMC for a quick sanity check on a single high-coverage genome; SMC++ for many unphased samples; MSMC2 for phased samples and cross-coalescence-rate / migration. Use one of these as a complement to Stairway Plot 2 - if both agree, you can move on.
Issues and warnings: All three are blind to recent demography (<200 generations) - pair with HapNe / GONE2 / IBDNe. PSMC and MSMC2 require phasing; SMC++ does not.
Reference: (Li & Durbin, 2011; Schiffels & Wang, 2020; Terhorst et al., 2017)

Purpose: Estimate Ne over the past ~200 generations
Specifics: Default tools for the recent-past gap that PSMC / SMC++ cannot fill. HapNe-LD works on unphased SNP arrays and is robust to admixture LD; GONE2 corrects the structure-induced overestimation of the original GONE. Use both and compare - agreement is a good sanity check.
Issues and warnings: Sensitive to recombination-map quality and to cryptic structure. Filter relatives upstream (e.g., with KING) - related individuals inflate apparent recent Ne.

Purpose: Likelihood-based inference of multi-population demographic models from the SFS
Specifics: When you want to test specific scenarios with confidence intervals - splits, migration, admixture pulses, growth. fastsimcoal2 is the workhorse; moments is faster and integrates with demes. demes provides a portable model specification; gadma2 automates the model search.
Issues and warnings: The model space is huge - a non-trivial part of the analysis is choosing which demographic models to fit. Always do model comparison (AIC) and parametric bootstraps for confidence intervals.
Reference: (Excoffier et al., 2013; Jouganous et al., 2017; Noskova et al., 2023)

Purpose: Test admixture hypotheses with f-statistics (qpAdm rotation, qpGraph)
Specifics: Default tool for explicit admixture-model testing. Run qpAdm with the rotation strategy (admixr) rather than a single set of right populations. Pair with TreeMix for visualisation if you have many populations.
Issues and warnings: Highly sensitive to the right-population set - rotation is essential. Report all plausible models, not just the lowest-p one.

Purpose: Genome-wide D, f4-ratio, fdM, df, f-branch on whole VCFs
Specifics: Default tool for first-pass introgression testing whenever you have more than three taxa. Dinvestigate gives windowed scans for localising signals.
Issues and warnings: All four-taxon tests are confused by ancestral structure and ghost lineages - do not over-interpret a single significant D. Validate with topology-weighting (Twisst2) and demographic modelling.

Purpose: Identify introgressed haplotypes when you have a candidate donor genome
Specifics: Sprime if you have a related but unadmixed reference population; IBDmix if you have the donor genome and want to avoid assuming a non-introgressed reference; hmmix if you only have an outgroup. Recent benchmarks (Witt et al. 2025/2026) show these only partially overlap - run two and take the intersection for high-confidence calls.
Issues and warnings: Validation is hard without ground truth; the recent benchmarking literature shows method-specific biases. Donor-free methods (ArchIE, ML-based) are a useful sanity check.
Reference: (Browning et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020; Skov et al., 2018)

Purpose: iHS / XP-EHH / nSL haplotype statistics
Specifics: First-pass selection scan from phased data. rehh is the easiest entry point in R; selscan covers the same statistics from the command line. Pair with a SFS-based scan (RAiSD / SweepFinder2) to triangulate.
Issues and warnings: Phased data required. Background selection and recombination-rate variation produce false positives - always interpret hits in light of the recombination map.

Purpose: Composite-likelihood scans for completed sweeps
Specifics: Complementary to haplotype-based scans - SFS-based methods do not require phasing. RAiSD is faster and includes a background-selection correction; SweepFinder2 has a longer track record.
Issues and warnings: Demographic mis-specification inflates false positives - calibrate with neutral simulations matched to your inferred demography (use stdpopsim). Both assume completed classic sweeps.

Purpose: Genotype-environment association controlling for the neutral covariance matrix
Specifics: Default tool for environmental-association scans. The C2 contrast statistic (Olazcuaga et al. 2020) tests adaptive divergence between groups defined by a binary covariate (native/invasive, host A/B) - very useful and under-used. Pair with RDA / pRDA for a multivariate cross-check.
Issues and warnings: Significance thresholds need pseudo-observed datasets. Pool-Seq is supported - mind the read-depth covariates.

Purpose: F/f/D-statistics, admixture-graph testing, especially for Pool-Seq workflows
Specifics: If you are working with Pool-Seq data (very common in non-model and conservation genomics) this is the natural f-statistics workhorse. Pairs neatly with BayPass for Pool-Seq selection scans, and replaces ADMIXTOOLS for Pool-Seq admixture-graph fitting.
Issues and warnings: Get the input class right (pooldata vs. countdata). Graph search returns multiple plausible topologies — bootstrap to compare.

Purpose: Multivariate genotype-environment association for polygenic adaptation
Specifics: Use classical RDA / pRDA as a baseline (Forester et al. 2018 pipeline), then RDAforest for non-linear environment effects and adaptive-neighbourhood mapping. These complement BayPass nicely - BayPass for SNP-by-SNP testing, RDA for genome-wide architecture.
Issues and warnings: RDA assumes linearity; RDAforest needs careful predictor selection. Both are sensitive to imputation choices.

Purpose: Forward and coalescent simulation under realistic demographic and selection models
Specifics: Always simulate. msprime for fast neutral coalescent simulation, SLiM 4 for forward simulation with selection / spatial models / arbitrary fitness, stdpopsim to start from a curated, peer-reviewed model for your species. Use these to calibrate p-values and to test inference methods on data with ground truth.
Issues and warnings: Forward simulations with realistic Ne can be expensive - rescaling tricks are standard. Stdpopsim catalog completeness varies by clade; check whether your species is supported.
Reference: (Baumdicker et al., 2022; Haller & Messer, 2023; Adrion et al., 2020)

Purpose: Tree-sequence-based inference and analysis
Specifics: If you want to do anything with ARGs - selection, allele ages, demographic inference - learn the tskit ecosystem. tsinfer + tsdate scales to millions of samples. Relate is a good complementary alternative for selection scans on inferred genealogies.
Issues and warnings: Learning curve is non-trivial. Topologies are more reliable than node dates; demographic mis-specification biases ages. SINGER offers Bayesian uncertainty if your sample size allows.

Purpose: Portable YAML format for demographic models
Specifics: Not a method - a file format. Worth knowing because msprime, moments, dadi, fwdpy11, GADMA2 and stdpopsim all consume demes models. Writing your demography once in demes saves redoing it across tools.
Issues and warnings: Limited to discrete-deme models with piecewise rates - continuous-space and complex selection still require tool-specific input.
